12:42 pm ET March 16, 2026 Share this update Trump says oil facilities survived Kharg Island strikes for eventual rebuilding Bart Jansen Trump said the strikes on Kharg Island on March 13 “destroyed everything on the island except for the area where the oil is,” with a goal to eventually rebuilding Iran. But he warned the oil facilities could be destroyed “on 5 minutes notice – it’ll be over.” “For the purposes of someday rebuilding that country, I guess, we did the right thing,” Trump said March 16 at an unrelated meeting at the White House. “But it may not stay that way. Just one simple word and the pipes will be gone too.” On Sunday, Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said Israeli airstrikes on fuel depots in Tehran "violate international law and constitute ecocide. Residents face long-term damage to their health and well-being."

12:24 pm ET March 16, 2026 Share this update Iran scorns Muslim neighbors for hosting US military Dan Morrison Top Iranian officials condemned their Gulf neighbors for hosting U.S. military bases, framing their ties to Washington as contrary to the spirit solidarity among Muslim countries. "Today's confrontation is between America and Israel on one side and Muslim Iran and the resistance forces on the other," Ali Larijani, a top security official, said in a statement posted on social media. "So where do you stand?" Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi joined in. "Hundreds of Iranian civilians have been killed in Israel-U.S. bombings, including over 200 children," Araghchi said in a post that suggested "some neighboring states which host U.S. forces and permit attacks on Iran are also actively encouraging this slaughter." Qatar, Bahrain, the United Arab Emirates and Kuwait all host U.S. military bases, and all have been showered with Iranian drones and missiles since the war began on Feb. 28. Araghchi thanked Pakistan for its "solidarity," and Iran allowed a Pakistan-bound oil tanker to pass through the Strait of Hormuz over the weekend.

11:25 am ET March 16, 2026 Share this update No relief until Israelis are safe, defense chief tells Lebanon Reuters Israel warned that displaced Lebanese driven from their homes by its military would not be able to return until the safety of Israelis living near the border was ensured. The warning from the country's defence minister came as Israeli troops pushed into new parts of southern Lebanon as it intensified its campaign against Hezbollah. "Hundreds of thousands of Shiite residents of southern Lebanon who have evacuated or are evacuating their homes in southern Lebanon and Beirut will not return to areas south of the Litani line until the safety of northern residents is ensured," Defence Minister Israel Katz said in a statement. Hezbollah, a Shiite Muslim group, attacked Israel in retaliation for the killing of Iran's supreme leader on February 28, the first day of the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran. Israel has responded with an intensive bombing campaign. The military has framed the ground offensive, launched after March 2, as a defensive effort to protect northern Israel from Hezbollah attacks, which it says have averaged at least 100 rockets and drones a day. More than 800 people in Lebanon have been killed, and more than 800,000 have been driven from their homes.

10:32 am ET March 16, 2026 Share this update See it: Jets land amid Dubai airport inferno

10:07 am ET March 16, 2026 Share this update Germany scorns Trump's call for help: 'Not our war' Bart Jansen Europeans have begun voicing their opposition to helping the United States protect shipping in the Strait of Hormuz, in a war they say they didn’t start and don’t want to participate in. "What does Trump expect a handful of European frigates to do that the powerful U.S. Navy cannot?” German Defense Minister Boris Pistorius told reporters in Berlin on March 16. “This is not our war, and we didn't start it." "Neither the United States nor Israel consulted us before the war, and ... Washington explicitly stated at the outset of the war that European assistance was neither necessary nor desired," German government spokesperson Stefan Kornelius said. Italy’s Deputy Prime Minister Matteo Salvini said sending military ships to a war zone would be interpreted as participating in the conflict. "Italy is not at war with anyone and sending military ships in a war zone would mean entering the war," Salvini told reporters. Spain said earlier it wouldn’t do anything to escalate the conflict.

9:36 am ET March 16, 2026 Share this update Trump could delay China trip, White House says Francesca Chambers Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said Monday that Trump could reschedule his planned trip to China at the end of the month for logistical reasons, walking back a previous suggesting from Trump that the trip depended on Beijing's position on the Strait of Hormuz. Trump said over the weekend that "we’d like to know before” the visit whether China would help with the strait before his trip. "We may delay,"he told the Financial Times. Bessent told CNBC's "Squawk Box," as he met with China's vice premier in Paris, that an overseas trip may not be feasible. “If the meeting for some reason was rescheduled, it would be rescheduled because of logistics,” he said. “The president wants to remain in D.C. to coordinate the war and traveling abroad at a time like this may not be optimal.” White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt confirmed to reporters later on Monday that Trump the trip could be moved. It was the latest signal from the administration that the war with Iran could carry into April. Trump is scheduled to leave for China on March 31.

9:30 am ET March 16, 2026 Share this update Trump calls on European leaders to do more against Iran: Leavitt Bart Jansen White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt told reporters that Trump continues to urge European allies to support the United States against Iran and to keep commercial shipping flowing through the Strait of Hormuz. “I think the president is absolutely right to call on these countries to do more to help the United States to reopen the Strait of Hormuz so we can stop this terrorist regime from restricting the free flow of energy,” Leavitt said. Trump “wished the UK had stepped up sooner and quicker,” Leavitt added. "But he continues to speak with our allies in Europe and is calling on them for support, just as he did when he called on them to step up with respect to their defense spending in NATO. He's calling them to do more here." British Prime Minister Keir Starmer said Monday Britain would not be drawn into a wider war in Iran but would work with allies on a "viable" plan to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. Leavitt referred questions to the Pentagon about Marines heading to the region.

9:17 am ET March 16, 2026 Share this update US 'dismantling' Iranian threat in Strait of Hormuz: Cooper Bart Jansen Adm. Brad Cooper, commander of U.S. Central Command who is overseeing the war, said the military has flown 6,000 combat flights against Iran so far and continues to dismantle threats to commercial shipping through the Strait of Hormuz. Cooper said on social media March 16 the strikes continue to target the production and storage facilities for missiles and drones, including a strike March 13 on storage bunkers on Kharg Island. He showed video of the destruction of a drone storage facility near the Strait of Hormuz, a drone production facility in Tehran and a command facility that produced torpedoes. Iran has nearly halted commercial shipping through the Strait of Hormuz with the threat of mines and missile attacks. But the U.S. military has destroyed more than 100 Iranian naval vessels, Cooper said. “We are also zeroed in on dismantling Iran’s decades-old threat to the free flow of commerce through the Strait of Hormuz,” Cooper said. “We are not done.”

9:03 am ET March 16, 2026 Share this update Oil steady, gas prices up as Iran economic impact widens Andrea Riquier Oil prices slipped fractionally even as prices paid at the pump ratcheted higher on the 17th day of the war with Iran. Brent crude, the global benchmark for oil and the price most affected by the fighting in the Middle East, was about 2% lower on Monday morning, near $97 a barrel. That’s a gain of about 44% since the start of the war. Gasoline prices follow those of oil, but with a bit of a lag. On Monday morning the national average was $3.683 a gallon, GasBuddy reported. In some areas, particularly the West, prices have been well over $5 a gallon for a while. That doesn’t bode well for consumers. Analysts had hoped for an economic boost from bigger tax refunds this year, but that’s looking less likely as the war drags on. “The oil price shock could easily offset the boost to spending power from tax refunds. The jump in gas prices alone will subtract a full percentage point from real aftertax income, if the current $99 (oil) price is sustained,” wrote Pantheon Macroeconomics’ U.S. team in a March 16 note. As of right now, futures contracts – the right to buy and sell commodities at a certain price – suggest the current shock will recede in the coming months. But if that changes, the damage to the economy could be more severe, Pantheon and others believe.

8:58 am ET March 16, 2026 Share this update House and Senate intel committees to hear about worldwide threats Bart Jansen The House and Senate intelligence committees will be holding their annual hearings to learn about worldwide threats, where attention is likely to focus on the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran. The House panel meets at 2 p.m. on March 17. Witnesses include Lt. Gen. James Adams, director of the Defense Intelligence Agency; Tulsi Gabbard, director of the Office of National Intelligence; Lt. Gen. William Hartman, acting director of the National Security Agency; FBI Director Kash Patel and CIA Director John Ratcliffe. The Senate panel meets at 10 a.m. on March 18.

8:53 am ET March 16, 2026 Share this update Treasury secretary walks back Trump's China threat Reuters It remains to be seen if President Trump's meeting with Chinese President Xi Jinping takes place as scheduled later this month, but any changes would be due to logistics amid the Iran war, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent told CNBC. Bessent's remarks come after Trump told the Financial Times on Sunday he may postpone a meeting with Xi if China does not help to unblock the Strait of Hormuz. The Chinese foreign ministry did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for comment. Trump is due to travel to China from March 31 to April 2 for a highly anticipated meeting between the leaders of the world's two biggest economies.

7:52 am ET March 16, 2026 Share this update Macron warns Iran against plunging Middle East in chaos Bart Jansen French President Emmanuel Macron joined the chorus on Sunday in urging Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian to allow shipping through the Strait of Hormuz. Macron also called on Iran to halt attacks against countries in the Middle East whether directly or through proxies, including in Lebanon and Iraq. “I reminded him that France is acting within a strictly defensive framework aimed at protecting its interests, its regional partners, and freedom of navigation, and that it is unacceptable for our country to be targeted,” Macron said on social media. “The unchecked escalation we are witnessing is plunging the entire region into chaos, with major consequences today and for the years to come.”

7:31 am ET March 16, 2026 Share this update Iran says strait is closed to US, Israel and allies Christopher Cann Iran’s Foreign Ministry on Monday said the country's armed forces are in control of the Strait of Hormuz and that the trade route will remain closed to the U.S., Israel and their allies. "The Strait of Hormuz will not be open to any country intending to harm Iran," Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said at a news conference carried by Iranian state media.

7:16 am ET March 16, 2026 Share this update EU weighs options as countries defy Trump's calls for help Christopher Cann Kaja Kallas, the European Union’s foreign policy chief, said its exploring option to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, including expanding its naval presence near the shipping lane. "It is in our interest to keep the Strait of Hormuz open, and that’s why we are also discussing what we can do in this regard from the European side," Kallas said ahead of a meeting of the EU's foreign ministers. Officials in Japan and Australia said their countries have no intention of sending ships. China has not commented on Trump's demands and threat to delay next month's summit in Beijing.

7:04 am ET March 16, 2026 Share this update Futures rise as tech stocks gain; Middle East conflict in focus Reuters U.S. stock index futures rose on Monday with shares of Meta among top gainers after a report said the megacap was prepping for sweeping AI-related layoffs, even as elevated crude prices due to the raging Middle East conflict kept risk-taking in check. Meta gained 3% in premarket trading after a Reuters report said it was planning to shrink 20% or more of its workforce to offset costly artificial intelligence infrastructure bets and prepare for greater efficiency brought about by AI-assisted workers. Keeping investors cautious were crude prices pinned at $100 a barrel, as shipments through the crucial Strait of Hormuz stayed mostly shut and U.S. President Donald Trump's demands for a coalition to secure safe passage seemed to be in vain.

6:55 am ET March 16, 2026 Share this update Japan has no plans to send ships to Strait of Hormuz Dinah Voyles Pulver Japan is among the countries Trump hoped would send ships to the Strait of Hormuz. But Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi said Monday the country has no plans to dispatch naval vessels to escort vessels in the Middle East, Reuters reported. "We have not made any decisions whatsoever about dispatching escort ships. We are continuing to examine what Japan can do independently and what can be done within the legal framework," Takaichi told parliament. 'This is a problem.' Iran war puts JD Vance in a bind ahead of 2028 Trump’s request for Japan to help protect oil and gas shipments puts the country in a difficult position because its war-renouncing constitution limits the scope of overseas military operations it can conduct, Reuters reported. It can deploy military overseas only to respond to existential threats to the nation. Takaichi told lawmakers she will travel to Washington, DC, this week for talks with Trump that will include the situation in Iran. Contributing: Reuters

6:46 am ET March 16, 2026 Share this update Trump accuses Iran of using AI to spread disinformation Dinah Voyles Pulver Trump on Sunday accused Iran of using artificial intelligence as a "disinformation weapon" to misrepresent its wartime successes and support. "AI can be very dangerous, we have to be very careful with it," the president told reporters on Air Force One shortly after he made a post on his Truth Social platform where he accused Western media outlets, without evidence, of "close coordination" with Iran to spread AI-generated "fake news." Over the weekend, Brendan Carr, the Federal Communications Commission's chief, threatened to pull the broadcast licenses of media outlets that run news related to the war in Iran that the Trump administration considers a hoax or distorts the truth. In a Truth Social post, Trump praised Carr's admonishment, saying he was "thrilled" to see the FCC "looking at the licenses of some of these Corrupt and Highly Unpatriotic 'News' Organizations." Republican Sen. Ron Johnson of Wisconsin and Democratic Sen. Michael Bennet of Colorado, and others spoke out against Carr's statements. In a post on X, Bennet said that it is "a direct attack on the free press in the United States. It cannot be allowed to stand." Contributing: Reuters

6:38 am ET March 16, 2026 Share this update Trump administration plans to announce coalition to escort ships through Strait of Hormuz: Report Kate Perez and James Powel The Trump administration plans to announce as early as this week that multiple countries have agreed to form a coalition that will escort ships through the Strait of Hormuz, the Wall Street Journal reported on Sunday. The newspaper, citing unnamed U.S. officials, reported that the countries are still discussing whether those operations would begin before or after hostilities end. USA TODAY has reached out to the White House for confirmation of the report, as well as to the leaders of the United Kingdom, France, Canada, and Australia for comment on their potential inclusion. The report comes after Trump took to Truth Social and called on other countries to send ships to the Strait of Hormuz, a vital 100-mile-long waterway that connects the Persian Gulf with the Gulf of Oman and the Arabian Sea. Iran announced the closure of the Strait of Hormuz days after the start of the war. Contributing: Charles Ventura, USA TODAY; Reuters

search_result.title: Oil prices jump after major Iran gas field is attacked. War updates
search_result.text:
11:23 pm ET March 18, 2026 Share this update Trump calls for end to strikes on oil and gas infrastructure Michael Loria President Trump is calling for an end to strikes on oil infrastructure after a flurry of attacks from Israel and Iran. Israel bombed Iran’s massive Pars natural gas field, causing oil prices to jump and marking a major escalation in the U.S.-Israeli war. Tehran retaliated with strikes on a refinery in Qatar, causing serious damage. "The United States knew nothing about this particular attack, and the country of Qatar was in no way, shape, or form, involved with it, nor did it have any idea that it was going to happen," Trump said in a statement. "Unfortunately, Iran did not know this, or any of the pertinent facts pertaining to the South Pars attack, and unjustifiably and unfairly attacked a portion of Qatar’s LNG Gas facility." Trump gave Iran an ultimatum around its attacks on Qatar, saying that Israel will no longer attack Iran’s Pars field but he promised the U.S. will bomb the field if Qatar is attacked again.

8:44 pm ET March 18, 2026 Share this update Ex counterterrorism chief denies claims Iran posed an 'imminent threat' Michael Loria Joe Kent, the former head of the U.S. National Counterterrorism Center who resigned in protest of the war, denied the White House’s claims that Iran posed an "imminent threat." "There was no intelligence that said, 'hey on whatever day it was, March 1, that the Iranians are going to launch this big sneak attack,'" said Kent, when asked on a political commentary show whether Iran posed an "imminent threat" to the U.S. "There was none of that intelligence." The former Trump pick to lead the nation’s counterterrorism operations came a day after his public resignation. His assessment of threats posed by Iran before the war is a direct repudiation of justifications made by President Trump for launching the war. Kent was speaking with conservative commentator Tucker Carlson.

8:41 pm ET March 18, 2026 Share this update Iran ally makes offer to stop attacks on US Embassy in Iraq: Reports Michael Loria A powerful militia group in Iraq backed by Iran has reportedly said it will halt its attacks on the US Embassy in Baghdad if the U.S. and Israel meet with a list of demands. The group, known as Kataib Hezbollah, has said it will stop targeting the embassy if Israel stops bombing the southern suburbs of Beirut and if the CIA in Iraq stops its operations outside the embassy, Reuters and CNN reported. In recent days, the group has been behind attacks on the embassy caught on video. The CIA is reportedly working to arm Kurds in the region in an effort to get the ethnic minority to join the fight against Iran.

5:13 pm ET March 18, 2026 Share this update Trump returns to Washington after dignified transfer Francesca Chambers Trump has returned to Washington from Dover Air Force Base, where he attended the dignified transfer of the remains of six U.S. service members who were killed when a refueling aircraft crashed in Iraq. The crew of the KC-135 crash were returned to their families on Monday afternoon. The transfer was private, and media was not present. Media coverage of dignified transfers is determined by the families of the deceased.

4:51 pm ET March 18, 2026 Share this update Wall Street sells off amid war, Fed decision Andrea Riquier Stocks tumbled after the Federal Reserve stood pat on interest rates, citing uncertainty from the war in Iran and concerns about inflation. The Dow and the S&P 500 fell to the lowest since November, down 1.6% and 1.4% respectively. The tech-heavy Nasdaq lost 1.5%. Bonds tumbled, with the 10-year U.S. note up nearly 6 basis points to about 4.26%. Bond prices and yields move in opposite directions: when yields rise, it's because prices are falling. Investors are selling because rising inflation erodes the value of the fixed income that bonds promise. That market action comes as prices for oil and gas remained stubbornly high. Brent crude approached $110 a barrel, Reuters reported, and the nationwide average for a gallon of gas is now $3.86, GasBuddy’s tracker showed. Meanwhile, Fed Chair Jerome Powell said inflation remains above target. The Fed believes inflation expectations are up because of oil prices. Expectations about inflation can be just as important as price levels themselves, because if consumers and businesses believe prices will be higher in the near future, they may spend more to try to get ahead of those increases, thereby creating a vicious cycle.

4:32 pm ET March 18, 2026 Share this update Trump looks for an off-ramp Susan Page

4:15 pm ET March 18, 2026 Share this update Iran revenge strikes cause heavy damage in Qatar Reuters Tehran struck Qatar and fired missiles at Saudi Arabia after vowing attacks on oil and gas targets throughout the Gulf in revenge for airstrikes on its Pars natural gas field. Qatar's state oil giant QatarEnergy reported "extensive damage" after the Ras Laffan Industrial City, an energy-industry hub, was hit by Iranian missiles. Saudi Arabia said it had intercepted and destroyed four ballistic missiles launched toward Riyadh on Wednesday and an attempted drone attack on a gas facility in the east of the country. The escalation threatens to worsen an unprecedented disruption to global energy supplies that has raised the political stakes for President Donald Trump. Diesel prices in the United States have already risen above $5 a gallon for the first time since the 2022 inflation surge that eroded support for his predecessor Joe Biden. The conflict that has already halted shipping from the world's most important energy-producing region and could now bring lasting damage to its infrastructure. Benchmark Brent crude prices rose around 5% to above $108. Stock markets veered lower.

3:57 pm ET March 18, 2026 Share this update Who attacked Iran's Pars gas field? Francesca Chambers and Cybele Mayes-Osterman US officials disavowed any role in the attack on Iran's Pars natural gas field as Tehran vowed revenge and several Gulf allies condemned the airstrikes. A White House official told USA TODAY the US was aware of the attack but did not take part in it. Capt. Tim Hawkins, a spokesperson for U.S. Central Command, referred questions to the Israeli Defense Forces. The attack was widely reported in Israeli media to have been carried out by Israel with U.S. consent. The Israeli military did not respond to requests for comment from the Reuters news service.

3:29 pm ET March 18, 2026 Share this update Intelligence chiefs grilled on Iran and global threats

2:59 pm ET March 18, 2026 Share this update Oil prices jump as Iran gas field is hit Reuters Iran's huge Pars gas field was hit on Wednesday, a major escalation in the U.S.-Israeli war, prompting Tehran to announce it would respond with attacks on oil and gas targets throughout the Gulf. Oil prices shot up after the attack in a conflict that has already halted shipping from the world's most important energy-producing region and could now bring lasting damage to its infrastructure. Benchmark Brent crude prices rose around 5% to above $108. Stock markets veered lower. Pars is the Iranian sector of the world's largest natural gas deposit, which Iran shares with Qatar across the Gulf. Iran's Fars news agency reported that gas tanks and parts of a refinery had been hit, workers had been evacuated to a safe location and emergency crews were trying to put out a fire. State media later said the fire was under control. Bigger tax refunds arrive just as gas prices surge nationwide The attack was widely reported in Israeli media to have been carried out by Israel with U.S. consent, though neither country acknowledged immediate responsibility. The Israeli military did not respond to requests for comment. Qatar, a close U.S. ally which hosts the largest U.S. airbase in the region, blamed the attack on Israel without mentioning any U.S. role. The Qatari foreign ministry spokesperson called it a "dangerous and irresponsible" escalation that put global energy security at risk. The UAE also denounced the attack.

2:15 pm ET March 18, 2026 Share this update Fed stands pat, nods to Iran war as economic risk Andrea Riquier Financial markets were little changed after the Federal Reserve announced its intention to hold interest rates steady, as widely expected. Stocks were modestly lower, with the S&P 500 off 0.6%, and bond yields slightly elevated, with the 10-year U.S. note off about 2 basis points. Fed keeps rates steady as Iran war, oil prices add pressure to the economy Brent crude was trading a bit lower, just above $102 a barrel, up about 3%. Gas prices were virtually unchanged compared to the morning, at an average $3.857 a gallon nationwide, GasBuddy reported. The Fed acknowledged the risks brought by the Iran war, noting that “the implications of developments in the Middle East for the U.S. economy are uncertain” in its official statement. Fed Chair Jerome Powell will take questions from reporters shortly.

2:09 pm ET March 18, 2026 Share this update Iran targets Saudi capital as foreign ministers meet there Saudi Arabia intercepted and destroyed four ballistic missiles that were launched toward Riyadh on Wednesday, the Defense Ministry said, just ahead of a meeting of regional and Islamic foreign ministers in the Saudi capital. "Scattered debris fell across various areas of the capital, with initial assessments indicating no casualties or damage," the ministry said. Several loud booms were heard and some of the city's residents received phone alerts for the first time warning them of a hostile aerial threat. The Gulf kingdom has come under attack from hundreds of Iranian missiles and drones since the U.S-Israeli war on Iran began last month, the vast majority of which authorities say have been intercepted.

1:55 pm ET March 18, 2026 Share this update Trump arrives at Dover air base for dignified transfer of KC-135 crew Bart Jansen Trump has arrived at Dover Air Force Base for the dignified transfer of the crew of a KC-135 refueling tanker that crashed in Iraq. He was greeted by Col. Jamil Musa, commander of the 436th Airlift Wing, and Col. Martha “Jeannie” Sasnett, commander of the Air Force mortuary affairs operations.

1:31 pm ET March 18, 2026 Share this update Vance backs Trump on Iran after Kent resignation Francesca Chambers Vice President Vance told a Michigan audience the president is "not interested in getting us in the kind of long-term quagmires" that the U.S. has seen in the past. "I know this president, I know the way that he thinks about America's national security, that is not a risk with this president at all," he said. Vance was responding to a question from a journalist about the "fracture" within the MAGA movement over the conflict following the resignation of Trump's former counterterrorism chief Joe Kent. Kent alleged that the United States went to war "due to pressure from Israel and its powerful American lobby" and that Trump had been duped by a "misinformation campaign" in a controversial resignation letter. "It's fine to disagree, but once the president makes a decision, it's up to everybody who serves in his administration to make it as successful as possible. That's how I do my job, and I think that's how everybody in the administration should do their job, too," Vance said to applause.

1:29 pm ET March 18, 2026 Share this update Stopping terror attacks ‘obsessive focus,' Vance says Karissa Waddick Vice President JD Vance said keeping Americans safe from terror attacks amid the War in Iran was an “obsessive focus of the entire U.S. government.” “We’re monitoring some classified stuff, some phone calls, things like that,” he said during a visit to Auburn Hills, Michigan. The comments came after Vance earlier addressed the March 12 attack on Temple Israel in nearby West Bloomfield Township, Michigan, calling man who rammed his truck into the synagogue a “terrorist.” Law enforcement officials are still investigating a motive for the attack. Jennifer Runyan, special agent in charge of the FBI's Detroit field office, called it a targeted act of violence against the Jewish community. Israel's government said the attacker's brother was a Hezbollah operative killed in an Israeli airstrike.

1:16 pm ET March 18, 2026 Share this update Gabbard drops statement that Iran's nuke program was 'obliterated' Cybele Mayes-Osterman Director of National Intelligence Tulsi Gabbard was scheduled to tell senators at a March 18 hearing that Iran’s nuclear program was “obliterated” after the U.S. strikes last June, according to prepared remarks shared online. Iran had made “no efforts since then to try to rebuild their enrichment capability,” the prepared remarks read. “The entrances to the underground facilities that were bombed have been buried and shuttered with cement.” "We continue to monitor for any early indicators on what position the current or any new leadership in Iran will take with regard to authorizing a nuclear weapons program," the remarks said. But Gabbard left that section out when she testified to the committee. Instead, she said that, prior to the U.S. war launched on Feb. 28, Iran “was trying to recover from the severe damage to its nuclear infrastructure sustained during the 12 Day War,” and continued to “refuse to comply with its nuclear obligations” with the International Atomic Energy Agency. Those comments do not appear in her prepared remarks.

1:11 pm ET March 18, 2026 Share this update US, Israel should have had a plan for Strait of Hormuz, UK official says Reuters The lack of a plan from the United States and Israel to deal with the partial closure of the Strait of Hormuz by Iran was "frustrating," Britain's Chancellor Rachel Reeves said on Wednesday. "It is frustrating that there doesn't seem to be a plan for what to do now after the very predictable closure of the Strait of Hormuz," Reeves told an event alongside Spanish Economy Minister Carlos Cuerpo. Fuel prices have jumped across the globe after Iran all but closed the strait to global shipping. The strait is the exit point for 20% of the world's oil production.

1:04 pm ET March 18, 2026 Share this update Gabbard dodges questions of whether Iran is 'imminent nuclear threat' Cybele Mayes-Osterman Pressed by Sen. Jon Ossoff, D-Georgia, at a Senate Intelligence committee hearing on March 18, Tulsi Gabbard, the Director of National Intelligence, would not say if the Intelligence Community had deemed Iran an “imminent nuclear threat,” saying instead that the president is “the only person who can determine what is and is not an imminent threat.” Ossoff responded, “It is precisely your responsibility to determine what constitutes a threat to the United States.” During Gabbard's testimony at last year's worldwide threats hearing, she said Iran had not decided to build nuclear weapons. President Donald Trump later publicly branded her testimony "wrong."

12:11 pm ET March 18, 2026 Share this update Iranians mourn Ali Larijani and slain sailors

11:37 am ET March 18, 2026 Share this update Investors cautious as oil jumps with Fed on deck Andrea Riquier The oil price pushed higher and stocks sold off on Wall Street as investors awaited the outcome of the March Federal Reserve policy meeting. Brent crude, the global oil benchmark, was up about 5%, just over $108, just before noon ET. The S&P 500 was off about 0.5%, and the 10-year U.S. Treasury note fell about 2 basis points as investors sold bonds. Yields – rates – on bonds move in the opposite direction of their prices, and prices have been falling as investors sell in anticipation of higher inflation. Those higher yields on benchmark government bonds can help nudge all kinds of other borrowing costs higher. But higher prices can also weaken the economy. Gas averaged $3.857 a gallon, according to GasBuddy, up nearly a dollar from a month ago. Fed meeting live updates: Will interest rate decision be impacted by Iran? Against that backdrop, the Federal Reserve has a tough path ahead. Inflation generally calls for rate increases to quell demand and bring prices back down, while a slowing economy may be boosted by interest rate cuts. That’s why most Fed-watchers expect the central bank to take no action on rates for the foreseeable future. A decision will be announced at 2 PM ET.

11:30 am ET March 18, 2026 Share this update Ratcliffe says intelligence does not support Kent's criticism Cybele Mayes-Osterman CIA Director John Ratcliffe told the Senate Intelligence Committee that he disagreed with Joe Kent, who resigned as head of the National Counterterrorism Center, that Iran “posed no imminent threat,” as Kent wrote in his resignation letter. Asked by Sen. John Cornyn, a Republican, if Iran had “ceased its nuclear ambitions” or its “desire to continue to build ballistic missiles capable of threatening American troops and allies in the Middle East,” Ratcliffe said, “Intelligence reflects the contrary.” “Iran has been a constant threat to the United States for an extended period of time and posed an immediate threat at this time,” Ratcliffe said. 'No friends but the mountains.' Kurds want Trump's help for Iran ground war

11:27 am ET March 18, 2026 Share this update Leavitt says Pentagon has 'tricks up their sleeve' to reopen Strait of Hormuz Bart Jansen White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt told reporters the Pentagon has “tricks up their sleeve” to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, but that Trump continues to call on European and other leaders to contribute more to the war effort. “The president is going to continue to talk to our allies, both in Europe and also in the Gulf and Arab region,” Leavitt said. “The United States military has a lot of capabilities, as you have seen play out over the last 18 days. They still do have tricks up their sleeve.” She said there is a plan but that she wouldn’t broadcast it in the media. “But of course, the president, again, continues to call on our allies to step up and do more,” Leavitt said. In Berlin, German Chancellor Friedrich Merz told legislators Trump's war looked like a losing proposition. "There is still no convincing concept today for how this operation could succeed," Merz said. "Washington did not consult us."

11:22 am ET March 18, 2026 Share this update 'Empty hall' or ready for centrifuges? UN expert says Iranian nuke facility is a mystery Reuters The International Atomic Energy Agency does not know the status of the new Iranian enrichment facility in Isfahan that is in an underground nuclear complex, agency chief Rafael Grossi said on Wednesday. "It is underground, but we haven't visited it yet," said Grossi, who is in Washington for a conference and to hold talks with Trump administration officials. Iran informed the U.N. nuclear watchdog of the new facility in June and Grossi said his inspectors were in Isfahan later that month to see it but had to cancel the visit when the nuclear complex there was struck at the beginning of the 12-day war with Israel. Grossi said because the inspectors had to cancel their visit, the agency does not know "whether it is simply an empty hall" or hosts concrete pads awaiting the installation of centrifuges - the machines that enriched uranium for power plants and nuclear weapons - or whether some centrifuges had been installed. "There are many questions that we will only elucidate when we are able to go back," he said.

11:16 am ET March 18, 2026 Share this update Leavitt calls Kent's Iran accusations 'insulting' and 'laughable' Bart Jansen White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said called former U.S. counterterrorism director Joe Kent's accusations that Trump was pressured into the war with Iran by a foreign country "insulting" and "laughable." She said Kent was not part of Trump’s daily intelligence briefings and wasn’t at the White House “for quite some time.” Kent, in his resignation letter, said Trump had been pushed into the war by Israel and its American lobby. “The president feels it’s deeply disappointing that after the president gave him an opportunity in this administration to serve the American people, that he would resign it with a letter filled with falsehoods, accusing the president of the United States of being controlled by a foreign country,” Leavitt said. “That’s both insulting and laughable all at the same time.” “Nobody tells him what to do,” Leavitt added.

11:01 am ET March 18, 2026 Share this update Gabbard sticks by Iran reversal Cybele Mayes-Osterman Gabbard, the Director of National Intelligence, stood by her support for President Donald Trump’s war in Iran, even after Joe Kent, the head of the U.S. National Counterterrorism Center, resigned on March 17, saying he “cannot in good conscience support the ongoing war.” Gabbard, a former Democratic member of Congress, was an outspoken opponent of a war in Iran during her 2020 presidential campaign and warned that Trump had pushed the U.S. “to the brink of nuclear war.” She later threw her support behind Trump’s 2024 campaign and was tapped by him to lead the intelligence community in his second term. After Gabbard testified last year that Iran had not decided to build a nuclear weapon, Trump said she was “wrong.” Gabbard expressed no differences with Trump over the Iran war. The president was “overwhelmingly elected” and “is responsible for determining what is and is not an imminent threat” she said on X after Kent’s resignation. Joe Kent's wife died in an ISIS bombing. Here's why he quit Team Trump

10:49 am ET March 18, 2026 Share this update Gabbard says Iranian regime is 'intact' Cybele Mayes-Osterman Tulsi Gabbard, the Director of National Intelligence, told the Senate Intelligence Committee at a March 18 hearing that the U.S. intelligence community assesses that the “regime in Iran appears to be intact but largely degraded.” “Its conventional military power projection capabilities have largely been destroyed, leaving limited options,” Gabbard said. According to U.S. intelligence assessments, “if a hostile regime survives, it will likely seek to begin a years-long effort to rebuild its military, missiles” and drone forces, she said.

10:47 am ET March 18, 2026 Share this update Warner slams State Dept response to Iran war Cybele Mayes-Osterman At a Senate Intelligence Committee hearing on worldwide threats, Sen. Mark Warner, the top Democrat on the committee, said the Trump administration’s cuts to the State Department were the reason that “thousands of American citizens were trapped in a literal war zone" in the Middle East "with little assistance from their own government.” “During a foreseeable security crisis—one instigated and chosen by this president—American citizens were essentially told: you are on their own,” Warner said in his opening statement. Thousands of Americans have returned to the U.S. from the Middle East, including some on charter flights arranged by the State Department, and more who flew commercial.

10:32 am ET March 18, 2026 Share this update Trump to attend transfer of six airmen killed in Iraq Swapna Venugopal Ramaswamy President Trump will be attending the dignified transfer ceremonies at Dover Air Force Base in Delaware on March 18 when the remains of six U.S. service members who were killed when a refueling aircraft crashed in Iraq are returned to their families. The airmen who died on March 12 include Air Force Maj. John A. Klinner, 33, of Auburn, Alabama; Capt. Ariana G. Savino, 31, of Covington, Washington; and Tech. Sgt. Ashley B. Pruitt, 34, of Bardstown, Kentucky, who were assigned to the 6th Air Refueling Wing, MacDill Air Force Base, Florida. Capt. Seth R. Koval, 38, of Mooresville, of Indiana; Capt. Curtis J. Angst, 30, of Wilmington, Ohio; and Tech. Sgt. Tyler H. Simmons, 28, of Columbus, Ohio were assigned to the 121st Air Refueling Wing at Rickenbacker Air National Guard Base, Columbus, Ohio.

10:21 am ET March 18, 2026 Share this update Poll: Trump loses Iran war support among independents Kathryn Palmer A majority of Americans disapprove of President Donald Trump’s handling of the war on Iran, according to a weekly poll from The Economist and YouGov. The three-day survey released March 17 found 56% of U.S. citizens objected to the way the president has been handling the situation in Iran, and another 36% approved. Another 9% of respondents had no opinion or were unsure. The figures show a drop in attitudes about the war compared to last week, when 52% disapproved and 39% approved. The pollster said the decline is almost entirely due to independents, as opinions among party lines remain entrenched. Almost all Democrats disapprove of Trump’s approach to Iran (92%), and a significant majority of Republicans approve (81%). The poll surveyed 1,595 adult citizens March 13-16, and has a margin of error of ± 3.3 percentage points.

10:16 am ET March 18, 2026 Share this update Trump eases shipping rule issues Jones Act waiver Karissa Waddick President Trump issued a 60-day waiver to the Jones Act , White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt announced Wednesday. The move, Leavitt said, is designed to ease disruptions to the United States oil market amid the war in Iran. “This Action will allow vital resources like oil, natural gas, fertilizer and coal to flow freely to U.S. ports for sixty days,” Leavitt says. The Jones Act is a century-old law mandating shipping between U.S. ports be completed by U.S. vessels. In a 2023 working paper, the National Bureau of Economic Research suggested waiving the law could reduce fuel prices. U.S. gas prices have surged to the highest level in three years since the start of the war in early March.

9:01 am ET March 18, 2026 Share this update Oil, gas prices rise with all eyes on Fed Andrea Riquier Prices for both oil and gas pushed higher as the U.S.-Iran war dragged on, testing the resilience of the American economy. Brent crude was trading around $106 a barrel at 9 a.m. ET on Wednesday, up a little more than 2% for the day. Gas averaged $3.86 a gallon nationally, GasBuddy reported, fractionally higher than on Tuesday. Most economists and analysts believe American consumers should have no problem managing an energy price shock of a few weeks. The economy is fragile, but not in a recession. What's more, many consumers stand to receive a bigger tax refund in 2026 than in past years. As hostilities drag on with no end in sight, however, the situation becomes more concerning. On Wednesday, the Federal Reserve will conclude a two-day policy meeting. There is virtually no chance that the central bank will make any changes to interest rates. Rather, investors and other observers will be keen to hear what policymakers say about the state of the economy now and their expectations for conditions over the coming months.

8:27 am ET March 18, 2026 Share this update Trump reminds 'fools' that Iran is No. 1 state sponsor of terrorism Bart Jansen With the Senate Intelligence Committee scheduled to hear about worldwide threats against the United States, Trump said “fools” should remember that Iran is the No. 1 state sponsor of terrorism. “We are rapidly putting them out of business!” Trump said on social media on Wednesday.

8:14 am ET March 18, 2026 Share this update Trump hints at leaving the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz to other countries Bart Jansen Trump, who has criticized European countries for not contributing minesweepers and other help to the war on Iran, questioned whether to leave them to reopen the Strait of Hormuz to commercial shipping. The strait is a key shipping lane for 20% of the world’s oil and Iran’s attacks on oil tankers have closed off a significant volume of shipments. While the shortage has driven up fuel prices worldwide, Trump has argued the United States gets only 1% of its oil through the strait and allies in Europe and Asia who rely more on it should step up. “I wonder what would happen if we ‘finished off’ what’s left of the Iranian Terror State, and let the Countries that use it, we don’t, be responsible for the so called ‘Straight?’” Trump said on social media on Wednesday. “That would get some of our non-responsive ‘Allies’ in gear, and fast!!!”

7:59 am ET March 18, 2026 Share this update McConnell blasts Joe Kent who quit over 'baseless' conspiracy theories Bart Jansen Sen. Mitch McConnell of Kentucky, who was a longtime Republican leader in the Senate, joined the critics of Joe Kent, who quit as head of the National Counterterrorism Center. Kent said Iran posed no imminent threat to the United States, which he said was pressured into attacking by Israel and its American lobby. But McConnell said Kent had testified in the Senate a year ago saying Iran and its terror proxies threatened U.S. servicemembers in the Middle East. McConnell said Kent pledged to lead with integrity and accountability. McConnell said on social media that people who mistake Kent’s “baseless and incendiary conspiracy theories for brave truth-telling are only fooling themselves.” McConnell said Kent’s resignation letter contained “virulent" antisemitism.

7:49 am ET March 18, 2026 Share this update Trump to attend dignified transfer of troops killed in Iran war Bart Jansen Trump will travel to Dover, Delaware, on Wednesday to receive the remains of troops killed in the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran. The event called a dignified transfer is scheduled for 1:25 p.m. EDT. At least 13 U.S. troops have been killed so far. Trump previously attended the dignified transfer of six troops on March 7. Vice President JD Vance attended the event for a seventh soldier March 9.

7:39 am ET March 18, 2026 Share this update Airlines continue to suspend Middle East flights Bart Jansen Airlines worldwide continue to cancel flights to and from the Middle East because of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, particularly at hubs in Dubai, Doha and Abu Dhabi. Delta Air Lines cancelled flights from New York and Tel Aviv until March 31 and postponed restarting a route from Atlanta to Tel Aviv until Aug. 5. Air Canada cancelled all flights to Tel Aviv until May 2 and all flights to Dubai until March 28. From Europe, Air France cancelled flights to Tel Aviv and Beirut until March 21 and to Dubai and Riyadh until March 20. KLM suspended flights to Riyadh, Dammam and Dubai until March 28 and flights to Tel Aviv until April 11. And Lufthansa cancelled flights to Tel Aviv through April 9, to other cities in the region until March 28 and to Tehran until April 30. Among Middle East airlines, El Al cancelled regular flights until March 21. Emirates, Etihad and Qatar airlines said they were reducing their schedules.

7:00 am ET March 18, 2026 Share this update Israel says Iranian Intelligence Minister Khatib killed overnight Reuters Israel's Defence Minister Israel Katz said on Wednesday that Iranian Intelligence Minister Esmail Khatib was "eliminated" in a strike overnight. There has been no confirmation from Iran regarding Khatib's reported death. Katz said he and Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu had authorized the military to kill any other senior Iranian official being targeted without the need for additional approval.

No injuries or damage to the power plant were reported in the strike on the Bushehr power plant located on the coast of the Persian Gulf, the organization said. Director General Rafael Mariano Gross issued a "call for maximum restraint during the conflict to prevent risk of a nuclear accident," the group said in a statement.
