You’ll be able to do that starting next week at Pittsburgh International. The airport is now poised to become the first U.S. airport allow non-fliers regular access into its gate-side terminal areas since security measures changed after the 9/11 terrorist attacks in 2001.

The airport has won approval from the Transportation Security Administration for non-ticketed customers to pass through security, though they will still have to go through the same screening as those catching flights. They’ll also be required to show ID so they can be vetted against no-fly lists.

USA TODAY: Pittsburgh International Airport Guide

“Participants should be prepared to receive the same level of security screening as travelers and should ensure they’re not carrying any prohibited items such as weapons before coming through the security checkpoint,” TSA spokesman Mike England says in a statement. “We look forward to working with the airport on this program.”

Need a news break? Check out the all new PLAY hub with puzzles, games and more!

Access with the “myPITpass” program starts Sept. 5, when non-flying visitors can request a day-pass at a special counter in the airport’s ticketing area. Access will initially be allowed only on weekdays between 9 a.m. and 5 p.m. Officials say that could be expanded if it the effort proves popular.

To ensure that non-flying visitors don't bog down screening queues, airport and TSA officials say ticketed passengers will receive priority in checkpoint lines during peak periods.

“We don’t expect this will have any impact on wait times,” England adds to Today in the Sky.

He says the TSA in Pittsburgh is staffed to handle any additional influx of visitors, adding no additional hiring was needed to accommodate the program.

When asked if the change in Pittsburgh could pave the way for similar efforts at other airports, England noted the effort was a local arrangement between the airport and the TSA staff there. Though the plan was OK’d by the agency, England says the Pittsburgh change “is not part of a larger nationwide initiative.”

Regardless of the details, the move is a major win victory for Pittsburgh International (PIT), which helped pioneer modern airport design when its new terminals opened in 1992. Anchoring the airport’s concourses was its “Airmall,” a collection of stores that was unusual for most U.S. airports at the time.

Even today, the Pittsburgh Post-Gazette notes “Airmall is home to several stores or restaurants like Hugo Boss, Armani Jeans, and Bar Symon that are not found elsewhere in Pittsburgh.”

ARCHIVES: Which airline is now the busiest in Pittsburgh? | Loss of US Airways facility 'a bitter pill' for Pittsburgh | US Airways continues Pittsburgh retreat

IN PICTURES: 30 cool aviation photos (story continues below)

In its early days, the retail area proved to be hugely popular with both local residents and with fliers. The airport even touted "street pricing" for Airmall stores, promising customers there wouldn't be a big mark-up just for shopping in the terminal.

“When the airport first opened, we used to come Christmas shopping out here because you have specialty shops,” local resident Tony Purcell tells Pittsburgh TV station KDKA while discussing the impending change in access there.

But post-9/11 security changes that restricted terminal access to ticketed fliers abruptly curtailed access to the airport shops.

ARCHIVES: Petition seeks to rename Pittsburgh Airport for Mister Rogers | Now allowed at Pittsburgh's airport: A bow and arrow | Southwest 737 diverts because of Pittsburgh air show

Pittsburgh’s Airmall was dealt another blow three years later when US Airways (now part of American) officially dropped Pittsburgh as one of its hubs, the first move in its decade-long downsizing there.

At the time, it was a stunning reversal of fortune for Pittsburgh. Just a few years prior, it had been US Airways’ busiest hub – and one of the busiest in the U.S. That hub presence meant Airmall was able to count on a steady stream of connecting passengers to patronize its shops.

But the death of US Airways’ hub in Pittsburgh sent passenger traffic – and the number of potential Airmall customers – into a tailspin that only recently leveled off.

ARCHIVES: Tears, tales and champagne flow on US Airways' last-ever flight (story continues below)

The just-announced change has left Airmall officials optimistic. PIT has occasionally offered non-ticketed fliers access to post-security terminal areas during special one-off holiday events. Those have been a boon for business, according to Ben Zandi, CEO of Fraport USA that operates PIT’s Airmall and airport concessions.

“The sales improve 100%,” Zandi says to KDKA. “I’m happy to get any increase; we’re optimistic.”

For Pittsburgh, the day-pass program also seems to have rekindled warm memories of the airport’s heyday as bustling airline hub.

"This is really, really cool," Allegheny County CEO Rich Fitzgerald said, according to The Beaver County (Pa.) Times. "It's going to make a lot of people happy."

Pittsburgh airport CEO Christina Cassotis, who has enjoyed a number of high-profile successes since taking the job in early 2015, touted the new program as a way to show off Pittsburgh’s highly regarded airport.

"It helps us showcase the airport for a lot of residents who haven't been flying lately," Cassotis tells the Times. "Everybody wins in this."

PHOTOS: A look at some of US Airways' airplane liveries from the past 35 years

YOUR TAKE: Boeing 747 photos from readers

TODAY IN THE SKY: United's special domestic Boeing 747 flight was a party at 38,000 feet

search_result.title: Coronavirus and cruises: Waivers, cancellations and itinerary changes
search_result.text:
Cruise Critic staff

Updated March 4, 2020, 2:02 p.m. ET

Many cruise lines have canceled or altered scheduled sailings to China and in and around Asia as coronavirus (also known as COVID-19) continues to spread around the world.

Cruise lines, working with the Cruise Lines International Association (CLIA), have swiftly reacted to the outbreak and started implementing their own precautions by canceling sailings, rerouting others, restricting passenger and crew boarding, and boosting screening procedures for passengers boarding if they have been through countries or regions impacted by the rapidly spreading virus.

Some lines, such as Viking and Avalon Waterways, have begun to allow customers to rebook cruises for up to two years after their planned sailing without incurring financial penalties. Norwegian is also making temporary changes to its policies, allowing passengers to rebook travel on other cruises up to June 30 and will not require final payment until 90 days before sailing.

Current travel advisories

The U.S. State Department has issued a level 3 warning (its second highest warning advising all but essential travel) for Italy and South Korea, though it issued a separate level 4 (do not travel) warning for Daegu, the epicenter of the Korean outbreak. Japan and Hong Kong are currently at level 2 (exercised increased caution). China, the epicenter of the outbreak, remains at a level 4.

Need a news break? Check out the all new PLAY hub with puzzles, games and more!

The U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention has issued its most severe warning (level 3, avoid non-essential travel) for Italy, South Korea and level 2 (practice enhanced precautions) for Japan. It has downgraded Hong Kong to its lowest threat level (1, practice usual precautions). China remains at level 3.

The latest: Diamond Princess passengers quarantined in Texas finally set to go home

Nervous about buying a plane ticket? American, Delta waiving change fees amid coronavirus concerns

Industry-wide travel restrictions

On Wednesday, all oceangoing member cruise lines of CLIA agreed to adopt additional screening measures to combat COVID-19.

The biggest difference? The new guidelines now include countries impacted after the trade group published its original guidelines on Feb. 7. In addition to China, Hong Kong and Macao, the list now includes South Korea, Iran and areas of Italy. They also mandates screenings for anyone who's been to an affected region within the past 14 days.

As a result of these changes, which became effective Monday, CLIA members are to:

Deny boarding to all persons who have traveled from, visited or transited via airports in South Korea, Iran, China, including Hong Kong and Macau, and any municipality in Italy subject to lockdown (quarantine) measures by the Italian Government, as designated by the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, within 14 days prior to embarkation.

Deny boarding to all persons who have traveled from, visited or transited via airports in South Korea, Iran, China, including Hong Kong and Macau, and any municipality in Italy subject to lockdown (quarantine) measures by the Italian Government, as designated by the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, within 14 days prior to embarkation. Conduct illness screening for all persons who have traveled from, visited or transited via airports in any destinations listed on the U.S. CDC “Coronavirus Disease 2019 Information for Travel” page within 14 days before embarkation. Illness screening includes symptom history checks for fever, cough and difficulty breathing in the 14 days before embarkation and taking of temperature.

Conduct illness screening for all persons who have traveled from, visited or transited via airports in any destinations listed on the U.S. CDC “Coronavirus Disease 2019 Information for Travel” page within 14 days before embarkation. Illness screening includes symptom history checks for fever, cough and difficulty breathing in the 14 days before embarkation and taking of temperature. Deny boarding to all persons who, within 14 days prior to embarkation, have had contact with, or helped care for, anyone suspected or diagnosed as having COVID-19, or who are currently subject to health monitoring for possible exposure to COVID-19.

Conduct pre-boarding screening necessary to effectuate these prevention measures. Enhanced screening and initial medical support are to be provided, as needed, to any persons exhibiting symptoms of suspected COVID-19.

Travel insurance and coronavirus: What travel insurance covers – and what it doesn't

Making travel plans? How coronavirus fear is spreading and putting trips in limbo

Travel waivers, cancellations and itinerary updates by cruise line

Read on to see which routes are being changed and which additional travel restrictions precautions individual cruise lines are imposing to combat the spread of coronavirus, as well as a list of companies allowing passengers to cancel or change their travel plans without paying a financial penalty.

If no travel restriction is listed for a given cruise line, that means it has not implemented restrictions beyond CLIA's updated guidelines from March 2.

AIDA

Itinerary changes: AIDA has canceled its entire Asia cruise season for ships AIDAbella and AIDAvita.

APT

Itinerary changes: APT's Paul Melinis, managing director for the U.K. and Europe, said, "No APT tours and river cruises pass through Wuhan, with our China itineraries starting at the end of March. In the meantime we are monitoring the situation closely through government travel advisories, the World Health Organization and our contacts within China. The safety of our guests and staff is always our top priority. We expect to continue operating all our river cruise and tours to China as planned."

Aurora Expeditions

Travel restrictions:Aurora Expeditions does not have any cruises in Asia but its ship Greg Mortimer is operating its maiden season in Antarctica, so the company has adopted guidelines set out by CLIA and IAATO (International Association of Antarctic Tour Operators). Since Feb. 13, the cruise line has been administering pre-embarkation health evaluations of all passengers and crew. with anyone registering a body temperature above normal not be permitted to board.

Avalon Waterways

Travel Restrictions: Guests who have been to any country covered under the CLIA guidelines within the 21 days prior to the start of their vacation will not be allowed to travel on any Globus tour.

Waiver and cancellation policy: Avalon is temporarily waiving all cancellation penalties until up to 30 days prior to departure, until further notice. Travelers with existing bookings and new bookings on Avalon Waterways can be moved to any related brand (Globus, Cosmos or Monograms) destination or itinerary in 2020 or 2021 without penalty, prior to final payment.

Azamara

Travel restrictions: In addition to following the new CLIA restrictions, Azamara will:

Conduct mandatory specialized health screenings performed on any passenger who has traveled from, to or through Japan, Singapore, Thailand, and Italy (all regions other than Lombardy and Veneto) in the past 15 days, and those who are uncertain about contact with individuals who have traveled from, to or through these regions in the past 15 days

Deny boarding to passengers presenting with fever or low blood oxygen levels in the specialized health screenings

Provide a full refund to any guests that are denied boarding due to these restrictions

Itinerary changes: None as of March 3

Carnival Cruise Line

Itinerary changes:

All South Pacific cruises on Carnival Splendor and Carnival Spirit that were scheduled to visit New Caledonia's Lifou, Mare or Isle of Pines ports have been diverted to other ports in Vanuatu.

Carnival is also reassessing stops in Grand Cayman and Jamaica following those countries' recent refusal to allow two ships – Horizon and Freedom – to dock there.

Celebrity Cruises

Travel restrictions: In addition to complying with the CLIA restrictions, the cruise line will deny boarding to:

Passengers presenting with fever or low blood oxygen levels during their specialized health screenings

Passengers who report feeling unwell or demonstrate any flu-like symptoms

In addition, specialized health screenings will be mandatory for:

Anyone who has been to Japan, Singapore, Thailand and all regions of Italy other than Lombardy and Veneto in the past 15 days

Anyone uncertain about contact with individuals who visited a country on either the denied-boarding or special-screening list in the past 15 days

All guests that are denied boarding due to these restrictions will receive full refunds.

Itinerary changes:

Celebrity Millennium: The ship's entire 2020 season in Asia has been canceled, including cruises departing March 14 and 28, April 10 and 21 and May 10.

The ship will reposition to the U.S. West Coast earlier than originally planned and offer a series of three, four and five-night Pacific Coastal sailings. From March to mid-April, the ship will be hosting a number of "Cruising for Heroes" sailings in support of California's firefighters, first responders and veterans. Later in April, the ship will sail roundtrip from Seattle prior to the start of its 2020 Alaska season from Vancouver.

All booked passengers on Celebrity Millennium will receive a refund, plus a 25% future cruise credit, as well as refunds of any excursions and onboard packages. Those with air travel and hotel booked through Flights by Celebrity will be refunded automatically; people with independent air arrangements may receive up to $300 per person reimbursement for any airline flight change fees incurred.

Celebrity Constellation:

The March 2 cruise is now a roundtrip sailing from Dubai and will skip Phuket, replacing it with a day at sea.

The March 17 sailing will now embark in Dubai instead of Singapore, and Phuket will also be replaced with a sea day, plus port times extended in Cochin, Goa and Mumbai. Passengers on these two March cruises will receive an onboard credit equal to the rate of one cruise day.

For passengers forced to change travel plans to embark or disembark in Dubai, flights or hotel booked through Flights by Celebrity will be re-accommodated, and the line will assist those who booked flights independently, with a reimbursement of up to $500 per person for any flight change fees incurred. Shore excursions booked through Celebrity for Singapore and Phuket will also be refunded.

Celestyal Cruises

Travel restrictions: In addition to the CLIA guidelines, Celestyal passengers who have been to China (including Macao, and Hong Kong), Japan, Singapore or Thailand within 30 days from their embarkation will not be allowed to board the line's Three Continents Cruise due to restrictions imposed by the Israeli public health authorities. Passengers should contact their original booking agent for alternative options.

Anyone who has been in the following countries or regions in the past 14 days: Japan, Taiwan and Vietnam

Anyone that has a temperature of 100.4℉ (37.8℃), a cough or difficulty breathing at the time of their pre-embarkation health screening



Costa is contacting passengers who are resident in these cities directly or through travel agencies to let them know and inform them about the full refund procedures.

Itinerary changes: The line has also canceled all shore excursions to the Piemonte, Lombardy and Veneto regions of northern Italy and any other city that may be added to the list published by the Italian Ministry of Health.

Cruise & Maritime Voyages

Travel restrictions: In addition to the CLIA guidelines, the company has extended the ban on passengers who have been in the restricted countries or regions from 14 to 30 days/

Itinerary changes: The line has now canceled further Far East Asia calls, including the replaced calls, plus Cambodia and Papua New Guinea. Crystal Cruises

Travel restrictions: In addition to complying with the enhanced CLIA guidelines, the line said that it will deny boarding to anyone with a temperature of 100.4℉ (38℃) or higher and/or shows signs of respiratory issues. India: No arrival visas for China passports

: Roundtrip Singapore March 31: Singapore to Ho Chi Minh City, Vietnam

Travel restrictions: On top of complying with the new CLIA guidelines, Cunard will conduct enhanced pre-embarkation screenings on passengers who have been to the following countries within 14 days of embarkation:

Vietnam

Singapore

Taiwan

Thailand

Japan

In addition, passengers should be aware of the following:

Although they may have passed enhanced health screenings, they may still be subject to active surveillance while on board and may be refused entry to port authorities.

Guests should be prepared to disclose documentation as evidence of their travel history.

Failure to disclose travel to affected regions is considered grounds for denial of boarding and possible legal action.

Itinerary changes:

Queen Elizabeth:

The ship's Asia season, scheduled to run from March 8 through May 15 has been canceled and replaced with an extended season in Australia.

The new season will comprise two six-night Tasmania voyages, two 11-night New Zealand voyages and an inaugural 27-night full circumnavigation of Australia.

Queen Elizabeth will then sail a 25-night Transpacific crossing from Sydney to Vancouver via New Caledonia, Hawaii, Los Angeles and San Francisco.

The ship's Alaska cruises are unchanged, and the ship will resume its Asia program in September 2020 as already published.

Queen Elizabeth’s Asia program will. resume in September.

Waiver and cancellation policy: "For those who have booked flights or additional packages directly through Cunard, all aspects will be refunded. If guests booked flights or additional packages via a travel agent or third-party tour operator, they should contact them directly. If any arrangements have been booked independently, guests should contact their travel insurance provider for details of how to submit a claim," the line said.

Dream Cruises

Travel restrictions: Dream Cruises is exercising CLIA-style restrictions on Explorer Dream, which is based in Australia and New Zealand for the summer season.

Itinerary changes: It is not yet clear whether Explorer Dream will return to Asia in March, but the line recently announced the ship will return to Australia for the October 2020 to February 2021 cruise season.

Waiver and cancellation policy: Dream Cruises will offer affected passengers who are unable to sail on Explorer Dream's March 8 cruise will receive full refunds of their paid cruise fare, port charges and shore excursions.

Emerald Waterways

Travel restrictions: While it is not listed as a member of CLIA, Emerald Waterways is complying with the group's guidelines.

Itinerary Changes: Currently, all itineraries, including those to Southeast Asia, are operating as scheduled.

Fred. Olsen Cruise Lines

Travel restrictions: In addition to complying with the CLIA guidelines, the British cruise line is conducting temperature checks at all turn-around ports. In a FAQ published to its website, the company said, "If a guest is displaying symptoms at this stage, they will be seen by our on-board Doctor who will assess their symptoms and decide whether the guest is fit to continue to travel."

During the last week of February, the cruise line said that health officials in the Dominican Republic had turned away one of its ships, the Braemar, after its captain reported that four Filipinos, two British citizens and two U.S. citizens were under medical observation for symptoms such as fever, coughing, or breathing difficulty. They have all since recovered.

"It is our opinion that this is an overreaction by the Dominican Republic,'' Fred. Olsen said in a written statement.

Holland America Line

Travel restrictions: In addition to following the CLIA guidelines, Holland America is also denying boarding travelers who have been in any location (including transit through airports) that were subject to lockdown at time of embarkation.

The following rules apply once a sailing is underway:

Holland America says staff will scan guest passports to determine whether passengers have transited through any prohibited areas. If they discover that any passenger has not disclosed travel through prohibited areas, that person will be forced to disembark at the next available port and may also face legal consequences.

Any guest or crew member showing symptoms of any respiratory illness while onboard will be subject to screening for coronavirus as well as potential quarantine and disembarkation.

Itinerary changes: MS Westerdam's March 14 and 28, and April 11 and 25 cruises, all of which were scheduled to depart from Yokohama, have also been canceled. New itineraries will be announced soon for Westerdam, which gained international attention when it was denied disembarkation by Japan, Thailand and Guam before being allowed to do so in Cambodia.

Waiver and cancellation policy: All passengers who were scheduled on the sailings listed above will receive a full refund, a 25 to 50% future cruise credit (depending on the departure date) and reimbursement of cancellation fees.

Hurtigruten

Travel restrictions: The Norwegian company says it is following guidelines and recommendations from the World Health Association), the International Association of Antarctica Tour Operators and CLIA.

Any guest or crew member showing symptoms of any respiratory illness after embarkation will be subject to screening for coronavirus and depending on their test results, potential quarantine and disembarkation.

Lindblad Expeditions

Travel restrictions: While it is not listed as a member of CLIA, Lindblad is following that group's guidelines.

Marella Cruises

Travel restrictions: The cruise line will deny boarding to anyone answers yes or is uncertain about any of the following questions on their pre-embarkation health screening:

Have you or any of your fellow travelers traveled from, visited or transited via airports in any of the following locations within 30 days of embarkation or did you have close contact with persons to whom this does apply? China (including Hong Kong and Macao), South Korea, Iran, Italy within 30 days before embarkation?

Did you or any of your fellow travelers have close contact to persons who have been placed on a leave of absence or issued a quarantine order?

Have you or any of your fellow travelers experienced any flu like symptoms (chills, cough, runny nose, or shortness of breath) in the past 48 hours?

Have you or any of your fellow travelers experienced diarrhea or vomiting in the past 48 hours?

Have you or any of your fellow travelers experienced any fever above 100.4℉ (38.5℃) in the past 48 hours?

Travel restrictions: In addition to complying with the expanded CLIA restrictions, MSC Cruises will deny boarding to guests joining MSC Bellissima and MSC Lirica in Dubai if they have been in Japan or Singapore. However, the restriction does not apply if they were traveling from unaffected areas and were only in the sterile areas of relevant Singapore, Japan and South Korea airports.

Guests and crew members who develop a fever or other symptoms after embarkation will be isolated in their cabins, along with their traveling companions and anyone who had contact with them.

Itinerary changes: The itinerary for the Grand Voyage, scheduled to run from March 21 to April 18 aboard MSC Bellissima, has been updated. The line has dropped calls to Shenzhen, mainland China; Hong Kong; and Keelung, Taiwan. The ship will instead call at Laem Chabang (Bangkok), Thailand; Phu My/Ho Chi Minh City, Vietnam; as well as spend an extra day in Kobe, Japan. The ship will call at Sir Bani Yas Island, United Arab Emirates, instead of Khor Fakkan, UAE, before sailing to Asia.

Waiver and cancellation policy: MSC has tweaked its cancellation policy to allow any cruiser who is booked on a Mediterranean cruise in March and April 2020 to postpone their cruise or switch to a different sailing operating in a different region and sailing within the next 12 months.

Norwegian Cruise Line

Travel restrictions: The CLIA member is abiding by that group's guidelines. It will also notes that:

Anyone who develops a fever of over 100.4℉ (38℃) or displays other respiratory illness symptoms while on board may be subject to possible quarantine and disembarkation.

Anyone who develops a fever of over 100.4℉ (38℃) or displays other respiratory illness symptoms while on board may be subject to possible quarantine and disembarkation. Some countries have enacted additional restrictions on who may not enter their ports and disembark, especially Chinese passport holders. The cruise line will notify impacted guests prior to embarkation.

Waiver and cancellation policy: Norwegian has made a number of temporary changes to existing policies, which it sent out to its travel agent partners with the following note: "We understand that your clients may be hesitant to plan or confirm their vacation, but we encourage them to get out there and experience the world aboard the best vessels at sea."

Noteworthy changes:

Final payment for all June and July voyages will not be due until 90 days prior to sailing.

Up until 60 days prior to sailing, the company will allow a transfer of the reservation to any other cruise that departs on or before June 30. The transfer voyage must be of equal or greater value, prevailing rates will apply, and penalties will be waived.

If a guest chooses not to sail, the name change restriction will be waived up to 45 days prior to sailing. Name changes will be permitted for the cruise reservation only and excludes flight arrangements through Norwegian Cruise Line and/or pre-purchased travel protection.

Guests with non-ticketed Norwegian Cruise Line air may make name changes.

Guests with ticketed air may make name changes but will incur a penalty and possibly air change fee, if either is assessed by the airlines.

Itinerary changes:

Oceania has canceled all cruises in Asia through June. All passengers booked on canceled sailings will receive a full refund along with a future cruise credit, valid for any 2020 sailing, equal to 25 percent of the cruise fare paid.

Given the extended quarantines imposed on the Diamond Princess and MS Westerdam, P&O is also advising passengers to do the following:

Pack enough prescription medications to last at least 14 days past the cruise's planned end date as well as a list of drug names, strengths and dosages in case refills are needed

Provide P&O with at least one contact who is not also on the cruise

Purchase a international plan for any cellphones they may be bringing

Purchase a travel insurance policy that allows cancellation for any reason
