search_result.title: Knicks vs. Spurs NBA Finals 1999 vs. 2026 - USA Today
search_result.text:
June 2, 2026, 6:24 p.m. ET

It was June 1999. Jennifer Lopez sat at No. 1 on the Billboard chart with her debut single. Star Wars: Episode I had been in theaters for a month and Austin Powers: The Spy Who Shagged Me was topping the box office.

The first season of The Sopranos had wrapped that spring and a New Jersey mobster with panic attacks was suddenly the most talked-about character on television.

Jalen Brunson was 3 years old and Madison Square Garden was buzzing. The New York Knicks were in the NBA Finals.

The San Antonio Spurs won the series in 1999; the Knicks haven’t been back until now.

Twenty-seven years later, the same two teams return to the NBA Finals in a completely different world.

Here are 10 things that did not exist the last time the Knicks made the NBA Finals:

The iPhone

Apple did not release the first iPhone until June 2007. In 1999, the cool phone was the Motorola StarTAC, the slim flip phone that fit in your pocket. Down on Wall Street, the shiny new toy was the BlackBerry, which had just launched that January as a two-way pager.

Neither had a camera. So nobody was snapping selfies at the Garden back in 1999.

Victor Wembanyama

The Spurs superstar was not born yet and the NBA had no idea what was coming. The tallest player in the league that June was 7-foot-7 Gheorghe Muresan, a Romanian center whose career was ending due to back problems and whose other claim to fame was a cameo in an Eminem video. The idea of a 7-foot-4 player who handles the ball, shoots 3s, blocks shots and switches onto guards was science fiction.

Social Media

No Facebook, no Twitter, no Instagram, no TikTok. If you wanted to scream about a Patrick Ewing dunk or a blown call, you had to yell at whoever was in the room and wait until you got to work or school the next morning to get everybody’s take.

Streaming

Netflix was a website that mailed you DVDs in little red envelopes. YouTube didn’t exist. Neither did Spotify. The 1999 Finals played on NBC, and if you missed it, you missed it. Maybe you caught the highlights on SportsCenter at 11.

Las Vegas Golden Knights, Aces and Raiders

Las Vegas was known in sports for boxing and betting in 1999, not being a sports town. The Vegas Golden Knights, the city's first major professional franchise, is in its third Stanley Cup Final, so it's hard to remember that they didn't exist before 2017. The Raiders left Oakland in 2020 and headed to Vegas. The Aces moved from San Antonio in 2018 and won the city's first major league title in 2022.

DVR

If you weren’t home for tipoff in 1999, you set the VCR and prayed the tape didn’t run out before the fourth quarter. TiVo had launched that March, but almost nobody had it.

Alexa

Nobody was asking a tube on the kitchen counter to play the Knicks pregame show. Amazon’s Echo didn’t arrive until November 2014. In 1999, if you wanted to listen to Mike Breen call the game on WFAN, you turned up the boombox or the kitchen-counter clock radio.

Modern video games

Gaming in 1999 meant you and your buddies on a couch passing the PlayStation controller back and forth. There was no Twitch, no online multiplayer on consoles, no streaming, no voice chat. Players in NBA Live 99 looked like floating heads on geometric bodies and NBA 2K didn’t launch until that November.

DoorDash and UberEats

Hosting a watch party for the Knicks games in 1999 meant actually having to call and talk to a person at the local pizza place to place an order. You had to scrape up the cash to pay and tip the delivery guys, too. DoorDash launched in 2013 and UberEats the year after. The closest thing you had to a delivery app in 1999 was a stack of delivery menus in your kitchen draw.

Legal sports betting

In 1999, if you wanted action on Game 1, you either had to drive to Atlantic City, fly to Las Vegas or know a guy. The Supreme Court didn’t strike down the federal sports betting ban until May 2018. There were no apps. No same-game parlays. No prop bets on Allan Houston’s first quarter rebound total.

search_result.title: 1999 NBA Finals rematch, how do 2026 ticket prices compare with ...
search_result.text:
June 2, 2026 Updated June 3, 2026, 9:37 a.m. ET

The San Antonio Spurs vs. New York Knicks NBA Finals series begins on Wednesday, June 3.

This guide compares ticket prices between the NBA Finals in 1999 to now and details the prices for every game of the upcoming 2026 NBA Finals.

The NBA Finals begin Wednesday, June 3. It's been 27 years since the New York Knicks last reached the NBA Finals, when they played, you guessed it, the San Antonio Spurs.

Given the excitement and longing surrounding the Knicks, tickets for the 2026 NBA Finals are in short supply, and that's reflected in the prices. It got us thinking—how much did various items cost in 1999?

Well, for starters, gas was around $1.22 per gallon while a movie ticket was around $5 to see some of the top titles like Star Wars: Episode I – The Phantom Menace or Toy Story 2. Almost makes me wish I was born a few years earlier!

SHOP:San Antonio Spurs vs. New York Knicks NBA Finals tickets

Needless to say, times have changed and prices aren't what they used to be. The real question is, how do ticket prices for the NBA Finals between the Spurs and Knicks compare to 1999?

Well, the answer may surprise you. Check out our findings below and how to buy the cheapest tickets to this year's NBA Finals between the Knicks and the Spurs.

How much were Game 1 NBA Finals tickets in 1999?

Game 1 of the NBA Finals in 1999 between the Spurs and Knicks was held at the Alamodome in San Antonio, and ticket prices to get into the event started at only $15.

Now 27 years later, a ticket to Game 1 on Wednesday starts at $801. Safe to say, $15 can almost cover a hot dog, fries and water now!

How much were New York Knicks NBA Finals tickets in 1999?

As astronomical as Knicks NBA Finals tickets are this year, 1999 was fairly affordable in comparison.

Court side seats only cost $2,700, which is a bargain considering get-in prices at Madison Square Garden start at $5,005 for Game 4. Anyone have that lying around in their couch?

2026 NBA Finals tickets vs. 2025 NBA Finals tickets

This year features two different teams in the NBA Finals compared to last year's Oklahoma City Thunder vs. Indiana Pacers matchup.

Last season, according to our archives, Game 1 tickets in Oklahoma City cost around $825, which is virtually the same as the $801 get-in price in San Antonio this year. The biggest change in price between the higher-seeded teams was Game 7, which saw tickets in OKC priced at $2,616 versus $4,254 for the hypothetical game in San Antonio.

Game 3, Game 4 and Game 6 tickets are considerably more expensive this season, in large part due to the Knicks being in the New York market. Game 3 tickets for the Pacers last season was only $797 compared to the $5,121 get-in price for Knicks vs. Spurs on Monday, June 8.

San Antonio Spurs vs. New York Knicks NBA Finals schedule

As the team with the better regular-season record, the Spurs have home court advantage throughout the NBA Finals and will host Game 1, Game 2, a potential Game 5* and a potential Game 7*. The Knicks will be home for Game 3 and Game 4, before potentially hosting a Game 6*.

Spurs vs. Knicks 2026 NBA Finals tickets Get ready for NBA Finals action near you! Get ready for NBA Finals action near you! Shop tickets now

San Antonio Spurs NBA Finals tickets

New York Knicks NBA Finals tickets

* = if necessary

When do the NBA Finals start?

Game 1 of the NBA Finals is scheduled for Wednesday, June 3 in San Antonio. Tickets for the game start at $801, making it the cheapest ticket of the entire series.
